item.title: Fareed Zakaria on the Moral Cost of Trump’s War
item.description: The foreign policy analyst Fareed Zakaria explains how the Iran war has been a turning point in America’s standing in the world.

item.title: Fareed Zakaria on the Moral Cost of Trump’s War
item.description: The foreign policy analyst Fareed Zakaria explains how the Iran war has been a turning point in America’s standing in the world.

item.title: Hillary Clinton: How to Fix Affordability
item.description: Our children will pay the price for the president’s indifference.

item.title: Why the Scary Inflation Numbers Might Not Be Quite So Scary as They Seem
item.description: I believe the gas tank is half full.

item.title: Gaza’s Rubble Is the Grave of Its Future
item.description: Six months after the cease-fire, ordinary Gazans contemplate their future while living with the wounds of war.

item.title: Vance Should Stand Up to Trump
item.description: Responses to Dana Milbank’s Opinion guest essay about Vice President JD Vance. Also: Melania Trump’s lament; a lost childhood; propaganda and facts.

item.title: How Haiti Can Dismantle Its Gangs
item.description: Force alone can’t stabilize a country in chaos.

item.title: This Is Why America Is Short Four Million Homes
item.description: The Federal Home Loan Bank needs to offer more loans.

item.title: Trump Is Turning America Into a Psychotic State
item.description: What are the implications if the government of the world’s most powerful country is chaotic in its thinking and not reliably in touch with reality?

item.title: The Last Thing Democrats Need Is More Policy Plans
item.description: Democrats need to stop being the party of wonks — and that’s no easy feat.

item.title: For Putin, Iran Is Something Close to Irreplaceable
item.description: Vladimir Putin has spent years building a coalition of the discontented on the premise that authoritarian states can outlast Western pressure. Iran is his proof of concept.

item.title: Contemplating Trump at 80
item.description: The president is giving old people a bad name.

item.title: We Are on the Cusp of a Revolution in Rare Disease Treatment
item.description: We can’t let the most important medical achievement of a decade slip through our fingers.

item.title: Trump Is Tearing at the Soul of the Military
item.description: If an unlawful order comes from the president himself, to whom does the soldier appeal?

item.title: How Ben Sasse Is Living Now That He Is Dying
item.description: The former senator wants to heal the America he’s leaving behind.

item.title: Artemis II Brings the Country a Much-Needed Sense of Wonder
item.description: Remembering the Apollo missions.

item.title: Why Republicans Will Not Run Away From Trump
item.description: Without Trump on the midterms ballot, how will his party fare?

item.title: What the Iran War Will Do to Your Life and Our Economy
item.description: The disruptions caused by the conflict will significantly affect the cost of living.

item.title: I Studied the Economic Fallout From World War I. This Could Be Worse Than We Expect.
item.description: Even if this cease-fire holds, the war’s shocks may last for years.

item.title: Europe Is Done With Appeasing Trump
item.description: Most Europeans, and much of the world, have concluded that no amount of flattery will win more than fleeting approval from President Trump.

item.title: Who Was Most Weakened by This War? Trump? Iran? 3 Opinion Writers Debate.
item.description: Will the shooting really stop? What should be Trump’s red lines? A discussion on where America’s war on Iran stands.

item.title: Did Woke Go Too Far?
item.description: On “The Opinions,” Nadja Spiegelman explores why careful language is falling out of favor.

item.title: What Dying Feels Like
item.description: What does terminal cancer feel like? The former senator Ben Sasse describes the complicated “algorithm” of managing the pain that comes with his diagnosis on “Interesting Times.”

item.title: What A.I. and D.E.I. Have in Common
item.description: A cloud of doubt casts a wide shadow.

item.title: Trump Pardoned Them. Now They’re Committing Crimes Again.
item.description: When President Trump returned to office, he pardoned about 1,500 Jan. 6 offenders. Since then, many of them have been charged with new crimes, including assault and child abuse. As the midterm elections approach, the Times editorial board argues that the Republican Party should pay a political price for prioritizing loyalty over public safety.

item.title: NASA Flew by the Moon, but Behind the Scenes, Its Science Is a Chaotic Mess
item.description: Without science, the stunning images of Earth from space are only pretty pictures.

item.title: Ditching Digital Junk Food for a Healthier Mind
item.description: Readers respond to an Opinion guest essay about digital consumption. Also: A failure of leadership; deconstructing Trump-speak; “moon joy.”

item.title: No Shy Person Left Behind
item.description: American democracy has a personality problem.

item.title: Viktor Orban’s Fate Is a Warning Not to Get Too Close to Trump
item.description: The Hungarian leader’s 16-year rule could be coming to a close.

item.title: Ben Sasse on How to Live While Dying
item.description: The former senator wants to heal the America he’s leaving behind.

item.title: Are These Woke Words Dead Yet?
item.description: On “The Opinions,” the editor Nadja Spiegelman and two culture critics debate which “woke” words are alive, dead or dying.

item.title: How Much Humiliation Can Vance Take?
item.description: The vice president gets saddled with the Iran war and eroding popularity.

item.title: Why the Strait of Hormuz Is Iran’s Trump Card
item.description: On “The Opinions,” the columnist David French says that no matter how much havoc Trump wreaks on Iranian targets, as long as Iran maintains control of the Strait of Hormuz, the country can keep going.

item.title: Israel’s New Death Penalty Law Is a Warning
item.description: An Israeli law aiming capital punishment at Palestinians but not Jewish settlers is immoral and unconstitutional and is part of a larger effort to suppress rights.

item.title: Twists and Turns in the Iran War
item.description: Readers respond to news articles about the Iran cease-fire and the road to war. Also: Betting on matters of life and death.

item.title: Anthropic’s Restraint Is a Terrifying Warning Sign
item.description: The rapid advance of artificial intelligence is happening now.

item.title: The Idealism of the Space Program Isn’t Enough
item.description: We’re racing a civilizational clock.

item.title: Did Wokeness Leave Us Worse Off?
item.description: The debate over words we can and can’t say.

item.title: A.I. May Worsen Wealth Inequality
item.description: A.I. will further enrich the winners and impoverish the losers, with inevitable societal impacts.

item.title: Deepfake Nudes Are Haunting America’s Teens
item.description: But a new set of lawsuits may finally hold tech companies accountable.

item.title: Now We Know What a Modern War Looks Like
item.description: The war in Iran is a real-time case study in the changing character of modern warfare.

item.title: He Was an Insurance Executive. She’s a Doctor. They’re Divided.
item.description: We put a doctor and a former insurance executive in a room.

item.title: Did Wokeness Leave Us Worse Off?
item.description: The debate over words we can and can’t say.

item.title: The Iranian Advantage Is an Illusion
item.description: And in Tehran, the truth should be clear.

item.title: Human Writers Who Rage Against A.I.
item.description: Readers discuss the ways writing and artificial intelligence don’t mix. Also: A degraded civil culture.

item.title: Trump Hasn’t Given Up on His ‘Unrequited Love’ for Putin
item.description: The president’s deference to authoritarian leaders has profoundly corrupted American foreign policy.

item.title: Who Really Controls Your Health Care?
item.description: In the first episode of Times Opinion’s new series “Divided,” a doctor and a former insurance executive confront a central question: Do insurance companies prioritize profit over patient care?

item.title: Who Really Controls Your Health Care?
item.description: In the first episode of Times Opinion’s new series “Divided,” a doctor and a former insurance executive confront a central question: Do insurance companies prioritize profit over patient care?

item.title: Health Insurance Companies Care About You. Agree or Disagree?
item.description: We put a doctor and a former insurance executive in a room.
